quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Angular velocity (ω)"?

options.text: τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m
v = √(GM/r); the speed needed for a stable circular orbit at distance r from the center of mass M
A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law
ω = dθ/dt; the rate of change of angular position, measured in rad/s; positive for counterclockwise rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: "Angular velocity (ω)" is defined as: ω = dθ/dt; the rate of change of angular position, measured in rad/s; positive for counterclockwise rotation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Angular acceleration (α)"?

options.text: The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout
x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
α = dω/dt = d²θ/dt²; the rate of change of angular velocity, measured in rad/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: "Angular acceleration (α)" is defined as: α = dω/dt = d²θ/dt²; the rate of change of angular velocity, measured in rad/s². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tangential velocity"?

options.text: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis
v = rω; the linear speed of a point at distance r from the rotation axis

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tangential velocity" is defined as: v = rω; the linear speed of a point at distance r from the rotation axis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Centripetal acceleration"?

options.text: Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r
a_c = v²/r = rω²; the inward acceleration required for circular motion, directed toward the center
U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium
Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d

quiz_questions.explanation: "Centripetal acceleration" is defined as: a_c = v²/r = rω²; the inward acceleration required for circular motion, directed toward the center. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rotational kinematics"?

options.text: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration
I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object
Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude
The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rotational kinematics" is defined as: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ω = dθ/dt; the rate of change of angular position, measured in rad/s; positive for counterclockwise rotation"?

options.text: Cross product
Newton's First Law
Angular velocity (ω)
Atwood machine

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Angular velocity (ω)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "α = dω/dt = d²θ/dt²; the rate of change of angular velocity, measured in rad/s²"?

options.text: Normal force on incline
Angular acceleration (α)
Gravitational PE (universal)
Kepler's Third Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Angular acceleration (α)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v = rω; the linear speed of a point at distance r from the rotation axis"?

options.text: Work by a spring
Apparent weight
Tangential velocity
Angular position (θ)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Tangential velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "a_c = v²/r = rω²; the inward acceleration required for circular motion, directed toward the center"?

options.text: Conservation of mechanical energy
Hooke's Law
Centripetal acceleration
Newton's Second Law for rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Centripetal acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration"?

options.text: Net force
Rotational kinematic equations
Rotational kinematics
Vector

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rotational kinematics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Rotational Motion is accurate?

options.text: Rotational Motion describes objects spinning about an axis using angular analogs of linear quantities: angular position θ, angular velocity ω = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α = dω/dt.
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Rotational Motion?

options.text: Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.
Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Rotational Motion?

options.text: The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
For constant angular acceleration, rotational kinematic equations mirror their linear counterparts with θ, ω, α replacing x, v, a.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Rotational Motion?

options.text: Forces and Motion applies Newton's laws to systems with multiple forces, including tension, normal force, applied forces, and weight.
The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.
Rotational Motion describes objects spinning about an axis using angular analogs of linear quantities: angular position θ, angular velocity ω = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α = dω/dt.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Rotational Motion?

options.text: In AP Physics C, the calculus formulation F = dp/dt can be applied to systems with time-varying forces, including rocket propulsion where mass changes continuously.
Real collisions are partially inelastic.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.
Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Rotational Motion?

options.text: Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Rotational Motion?

options.text: Hard drive engineering specifying angular velocity (7200 RPM) and computing read/write head linear speed at different radii
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Rotational Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Rocket equation derivation: integrating F = dp/dt for variable mass to find the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation Δv = v_e × ln(m₀/m_f)
Tracking projectile trajectories in sports science (e.g., baseball launch angle optimization) using one-dimensional kinematic decomposition
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Rotational Motion?

options.text: Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Rotational Motion in practice?

options.text: Hard drive engineering specifying angular velocity (7200 RPM) and computing read/write head linear speed at different radii
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Motion.
